does: This paper identifies and systematically studies time-of-check to time-of-use (TOCTOU) vulnerabilities in LLM-enabled agents, a security gap that arises when agents validate external state that is subsequently modified before use. The authors introduce TOCTOU-Bench, a benchmark comprising 66 realistic user tasks designed to evaluate agent susceptibility to these vulnerabilities. They propose and evaluate three countermeasures adapted from systems security: prompt rewriting to encourage agents to re-validate state before use, state integrity monitoring to detect unauthorized modifications, and tool-fusing to reduce windows of vulnerability. The study demonstrates that existing agents are vulnerable to TOCTOU attacks and that their proposed mitigations achieve up to 25% detection accuracy, revealing the challenge of defending agentic workflows against this class of attack.
connects: This work extends the emerging security analysis of LLM-enabled agents, building on prior research into prompt injection attacks and data exfiltration threats that have characterized early vulnerability studies in this space. It translates classical TOCTOU vulnerabilities from systems and operating systems security into the agentic setting, where the interaction between language models, external tools, and dynamically changing world state creates new attack surfaces. The paper connects to broader work on agent reliability and robustness by highlighting how the sequential decision-making of agents creates temporal windows of vulnerability absent in static language model interactions. It also draws on security verification and runtime monitoring techniques from systems security, adapting them to the constraints and characteristics of LLM-based agents.
enables: This work opens up systematic research into temporal vulnerabilities in agentic systems and establishes TOCTOU-Bench as a standard evaluation resource for the community. It points toward developing stronger verification and monitoring techniques specifically designed for agentic workflows, where the gap between checking and using is inherent to the agent's planning and execution cycle. The findings motivate future work on provably safe agent architectures that maintain state consistency guarantees, formal verification methods for agent behavior under concurrent modifications, and stronger integration of cryptographic techniques for state integrity into agent tool-use. More broadly, this research sets a foundation for understanding how classical computer security vulnerabilities manifest in the era of autonomous LLM-enabled agents and informs the design of more robust agent frameworks.
